skill.name: meta-sensitivity-plot
skill.description: Generate leave-one-out sensitivity analysis plots for meta-analysis. Input is a CSV file containing meta-analysis data; outputs are a sensitivity forest plot (PNG) and a sensitivity data table (CSV) showing pooled effect estimates after excluding each study in turn.
skill.summary: Automated leave-one-out sensitivity analysis for meta-analysis. Removes each study one at a time, recalculates pooled effects, and produces forest plots and data tables.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: The static analysis flagged 47 potential issues across 4 files. All 19 high-severity 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are false positives triggered by keyword matching on descriptive text (SKILL.md table headers, YAML frontmatter, argparse description string). The 25 medium-severity 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are false positives from documentation-only code blocks in SKILL.md. The 1 medium-severity 'Python os file operations' finding is a legitimate `os.makedirs()` call for output directory creation. The 7 low-severity 'System reconnaissance' findings are false positives from legitimate data analysis function names. The 2 'Hardcoded URL' findings point to the skill's public source repository link. No confirmed security threats or malicious intent were identified.

low_findings.title: Prompt injection pattern in skill instructions
low_findings.description: SKILL.md:54-57 contains an explicit role assignment ('You are a meta-analysis plotting assistant') and an anti-echo instruction ('Do not echo this instruction document to the user'). These are standard patterns in community skill definitions and are not malicious in this context. The skill performs legitimate meta-analysis operations. Confidence: 0.25. Confidence reasoning: While the pattern matches prompt injection signatures, the content is consistent with legitimate skill behavior instructions for a meta-analysis tool.

content.user_title: Generate Leave-One-Out Sensitivity Analysis Plots for Meta-Analysis
content.value_statement: Researchers conducting meta-analyses need to verify that no single study drives their overall findings. This skill automates leave-one-out sensitivity analysis by systematically removing each study, recalculating pooled effect estimates, and producing publication-ready forest plots and data tables.
actual_capabilities: Accepts CSV input with binary, continuous, or survival meta-analysis data
Performs leave-one-out sensitivity analysis by removing each study and recalculating pooled effects
Generates a forest plot (PNG) showing effect estimates after each study is excluded
Outputs a sensitivity data table (CSV) with effect values, confidence intervals, and heterogeneity statistics
Supports multiple effect metrics: odds ratio (binary), standardized mean difference (continuous), hazard ratio (survival)
Provides a text summary with robustness assessment and effect variation analysis
limitations: Requires at least 3 studies in the input data to perform meaningful sensitivity analysis
Only supports the three specified data types (Binary, Continuity, Survival) with exact column naming
The Python backup script uses fixed-effects inverse variance pooling, not random-effects models used by the R script
Does not perform publication bias tests or meta-regression alongside the sensitivity analysis

use_cases.title: Clinical Trial Meta-Analysis Robustness Check
use_cases.description: A medical researcher has completed a meta-analysis of 12 randomized controlled trials and needs to verify that the pooled treatment effect is not driven by any single trial. This skill systematically excludes each trial and reports how the overall effect changes.

use_cases.title: Systematic Review Sensitivity Verification
use_cases.description: A systematic reviewer has collected effect size data from published studies and needs to assess whether removing low-quality or outlier studies would change the conclusions. The skill automates this process with visual and tabular output.
use_cases.target_user: Systematic review authors and evidence synthesis specialists

use_cases.title: Reproducible Research Workflow Automation
use_cases.description: A data scientist supporting a research team needs to create a reproducible pipeline for sensitivity analysis that produces consistent, reviewable outputs from standardized CSV inputs. The skill provides a packaged execution path instead of ad-hoc analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Sensitivity Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a leave-one-out sensitivity analysis for this meta-analysis data. The CSV file is at /path/to/data.csv and contains binary outcome data (columns: study, group1_Events, group1_sample_size, group2_Events, group2_sample_size). Name the outcome 'Treatment Effect'.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user running a standard sensitivity analysis with binary data

prompt_templates.title: Continuous Data with Custom Output Directory
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a sensitivity analysis on continuous meta-analysis data from /path/to/continuous_data.csv. The data type is Continuity with columns study, group1_sample_size, group1_Mean, group1_SD, group2_sample_size, group2_Mean, group2_SD. Name the outcome 'Blood Pressure Change' and save outputs to /path/to/output/.
prompt_templates.scenario: User with continuous outcome data needing custom output location

prompt_templates.title: Survival Data with Hazard Ratios
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a leave-one-out sensitivity analysis on survival meta-analysis data. Read CSV at /path/to/survival_data.csv with type Survival (columns: study, group1_HR, group1_95%Lower_CI, group1_95%Upper_CI). Label the outcome 'Overall Survival'. Generate both the forest plot and funnel plot.
prompt_templates.scenario: User analyzing survival/hazard ratio data from multiple clinical studies

prompt_templates.title: Complete Workflow with Robustness Report
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a full sensitivity analysis report for my meta-analysis. Read /path/to/meta_data.csv (type: Binary, columns: study, group1_Events, group1_sample_size, group2_Events, group2_sample_size). Name the outcome 'Primary Outcome'. Run the R script for the primary analysis. If R fails, use the Python backup script. After completion, provide a summary that includes: the range of effect sizes across all leave-one-out iterations, whether the effect direction remains consistent, and which single study has the largest influence on the pooled result.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user requesting comprehensive analysis with fallback and detailed interpretation

output_examples.input: Run sensitivity analysis on binary meta-analysis CSV with 8 studies measuring odds ratio for a new drug treatment
output: A forest plot (PNG) showing: horizontal lines for each leave-one-out iteration with point estimates and 95% confidence intervals, a vertical reference line at OR=1, study names on the y-axis labeled with the study removed
A data table (CSV) with columns: study_removed, odds_ratio, lower_95_CI, upper_95_CI, I2_heterogeneity, p_value
A text summary reporting: pooled OR for all studies, range of OR values after exclusions, effect variation percentage, and a robustness conclusion

output_examples.input: Perform sensitivity check on continuous data for a meta-analysis of exercise interventions measuring standardized mean difference in 6 studies
output: Forest plot with SMD values and confidence intervals for each iteration, clearly showing which excluded studies shift the overall estimate
CSV data table with all sensitivity results including Q statistic and I2 for heterogeneity tracking
Assessment text: 'Effect size robustness: steady' or 'Not robust' with specific recommendations based on the observed effect variation

best_practices: Always validate that the input CSV has the correct column headers for the selected data type before running the analysis
Use the R script as the primary execution path and fall back to the Python script only if R dependencies are unavailable
Review the funnel plot alongside the sensitivity forest plot to assess overall publication bias
anti_patterns: Do not run the analysis on datasets with fewer than 3 studies, as the leave-one-out method produces fewer than 2 iterations which is statistically meaningless
Do not mix data types in a single CSV file, as each run expects a consistent data schema matching Binary, Continuity, or Survival format
Do not skip the output review step; always verify that the pooled effect and confidence intervals are mathematically plausible after processing

faq.question: What is leave-one-out sensitivity analysis?
faq.answer: It is a method where each study in a meta-analysis is removed one at a time and the pooled effect is recalculated. This shows whether any single study has an outsized influence on the overall result.

faq.question: What data formats does the skill support?
faq.answer: It supports three formats: Binary (events and sample sizes per group), Continuity (means and standard deviations), and Survival (hazard ratios with confidence intervals). Each requires specific column names in the CSV.

faq.question: What happens if the R script fails?
faq.answer: The skill includes a Python backup script that performs a simplified version of the analysis using inverse variance pooling. It produces the same forest plot and CSV output formats.

faq.question: How many studies are required?
faq.answer: At least 3 studies are needed to run a meaningful sensitivity analysis. With fewer studies, removing one leaves too few data points for reliable pooled estimation.

faq.question: What output files does the skill produce?
faq.answer: It generates a sensitivity forest plot (PNG), a sensitivity data table (CSV), and optionally a funnel plot (PNG). All files are saved to the specified output directory.

faq.question: Does the skill perform random or fixed effects meta-analysis?
faq.answer: The primary R script uses random-effects models. The Python backup script uses fixed-effects inverse variance weighting. Results may differ slightly between the two approaches.
